author: @margaretjohnson4413
text: Interesting conversion. I watch some homesteading videos and interestingly the Amish have a hand powered washing tub with a hand powered wringer. Of course this takes up more time. The electric powered machine does not. It would be interesting to see a conversion using a mechanical timer to disengage the tub to end the wash time and even better one with timer and gearing to make a spin cycle before disengaging. Complicated I know and what to do about the water discharge would be possibly the most contentious and complicated to avoid land contamination.

author: @nickwinn7812
text: When the drum moves about on it's suspension system, the drive belt will fall off, because the drum will be moving around whist the drive spindle is static. The drum must change direction every few seconds to prevent the clothes from tangling up - how do you propose to do this? The average washing machine motor is around 350W (1/2 hp) that would be too much for human power. To make this work you need to also consider the filling emptying and heating of the water. Please re-do this video covering these issues, so that we can actually do something useful.

author: @hendrikhardeman9832
text:
07:16 That's not how one pronounces Miele.
The correct pronunciation of Miele is roughly MEE-leh or MEE-luh. It is difficult to transcribe how you pronounce Miele phonetically because there is not an exact correspondence in English. The first syllable, which is stressed, is simple, it is exactly pronounced like the word "me". The "e" in the second syllable, -le, is pronounced similarly to the "a" in the word "about". The result is that the -le is pronounced with a sound that is somewhere between -leh and -luh

author: @barrychatwin661

author: @michaelcasper4727
text: The only tradeoff with this conversion is the loss of features.  The controls open solenoids for hot, warm or cold water, time the wash session, empty the wash for the rinse cycle, time the rinse cycle, drain and spin cycle the wash.  You still have to run the water solenoids to get water in - unless you fancy pouring a bucket of water into a side-loader.  In fact, the motor was very efficient, so the washer was a prime candidate for a solar generator.  Luke just has to pedal the bike generator (made in an earlier video) to keep a charge in the solar generator to offset the washer load as it runs.  If Luke does really well, he can take breaks between cycles.

replies.author: @michaelcasper4727
replies.text: Oh, forgot about the drain solenoid - even if you can heave a bucket of water into the side loader without spilling a drop, how do you get it out?  And is there a little "pumping action" to entice the water to leave?

author: @rickytleung
text: Canadian here, I swear by the front loader design. Mechanically simpler and just more reliable. When my brother and I moved out and we bought new washer and dryer for our laundry room, he went out and bought a top loader washer because of the sales person's marketing mumbo jumbo. I told him that why didn't he ask me about the washer before he bought it and I told him with the amount of laundry that he has to do as a massage therapist, the front loader is not going to last. Lo and behold, my brother loaded the washer with a batch of linens and didn't spread them out to balance out the front loader drum and the drum was so off balanced that I'm sure it would have self destructed if I didn't go down to the laundry room to stop the wash cycle. There was a moment of "I told you so" and we replaced the washer with a front loader.

author: @xanataph
text:
Our washing machine at home has been running for years from alternative power. It's plugged into an inverter that runs from our off-grid solar power system. No faffing about with bicycle wheels! lol If you want the automatic washer to stay automatic, it would be very complicated to remove all the electrics and have a purely mechanical system. Nevertheless it could be done. 

However, there is piece of gear in these machines that is not really needed. That's the drain pump. This is really only a convenience so your drain hose can raise up to discharge into a tub etc. A solenoid valve would probably use less energy and the washer will drain quite happily by gravity if the drain outlet is below the level of the bowl or drum.  After all, we don't put the drains of sinks etc above the level of the sink and then run a pump to empty it! 

Our dishwasher is a slightly different matter though. It runs as a standard electric dishwasher from an inverter except for one thing. The heating element is disconnected. However, unlike laundry, having hot water is essential for doing dishes. I removed the solenoid valve and this now controls cold water feeding into a fairly large plastic solar thermal panel that once was part of a pool heating system. 

When the cold water is turned on it feeds into the bottom and pushes the hot water out of the top of this panel through a hose and into the dishwasher. Thus the solar panel is set up as an open system batch heater. It works well, and as long as you carefully choose the time of day it'll produce hot water in all Seasons for the dishes. In Spring and Summer of course you don't have to be so fussy.

author: @miken7629
text:
I thought a universal motor could run on either AC or DC.  Reconfigure motor for DC and you can run it with a solar panel.    More than likely, the controller board actually runs on DC that is being produced by a voltage regulator.  

On my sailboat, I have a redneck washing machine.   It consist of a 5 gallon bucket and a toilet plunger as an agitator.
